topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone screen, a stark intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon study session. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched, the air suddenly thick in my lungs. I froze, my fingers hovering over the screen, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. I slowly turned my head, expecting to see him, but the empty classroom felt cavernous. A cold trickle of sweat traced a path down my spine. Who would be sending this? My mind raced, cataloging every possible scenario, none of them comforting. I quickly minimized the message, my hands trembling slightly as I tried to regain a semblance of composure. The silence of the room seemed to amplify the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 2]

I found the profile picture first—a slightly blurred photo of a park bench, something innocuous. Then, the message followed, a casual query about an essay I had submitted weeks ago. A wave of nausea washed over me. I gripped my desk so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every instinct screamed at me to slam the phone down and flee the room, but my feet felt cemented to the floor. I stared at the screen, rereading the polite, yet unnerving, text. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy residue. The fluorescent lights above seemed too bright, too harsh, illuminating the sheer panic tightening my throat.

[story 3]

It happened while I was scrolling through my feed, a completely unrelated distraction. Then, there it was: a comment on a post I had briefly shared about a historical topic—a comment from Mr. Harrison. My entire body stiffened. I dropped my phone onto my lap, the small clatter echoing loudly in the otherwise deserted library. I couldn't move. My gaze was locked on the screen, a knot of tension coiling tight in my stomach. I forced myself to look up, scanning the rows of silent students, searching for any sign that this was a shared hallucination. The reality of the situation—the silent, undeniable connection—made my palms slick with perspiration. I wanted to disappear.
